prompt: Allons demander la clé de notre chambre.
hint: Where do you go to get a room key at a hotel?
explanation: La réception is the reception/front desk -- the place in a hotel where you check in and collect your room key.
reviewPrompt: Where in a hotel would you ask for your room key?

prompt: Notre table est près de la fenêtre.
hint: Where would you find a table near a window?
explanation: Le restaurant is where you sit at a table (une table) and eat. 'Près de la fenêtre' means near the window.
reviewPrompt: What does 'Notre table est près de la fenêtre' tell you about the location?

prompt: Tu peux garer ta voiture ici.
hint: 'Garer ta voiture' means to park your car.
explanation: Le garage is where you park (garer) your car (voiture). 'Tu peux garer ta voiture ici' means 'You can park your car here.'
reviewPrompt: What does 'garer ta voiture' mean?

prompt: Notre chambre est au 6ème étage.
hint: If your room is on the 6th floor, what would help you get there?
explanation: L'ascenseur is the lift/elevator. When your room (chambre) is on the 6th floor (6ème étage), you would use the lift!
reviewPrompt: What is 'l'ascenseur' and when would you need it?

prompt: ______ cette aspirine si vous êtes malade. [prendre]
hint: This is the polite/plural command form (vous) of prendre.
explanation: Prenez is the vous form of prendre (to take). In a polite instruction like 'Take this aspirin if you are ill', we use the vous imperative.
reviewPrompt: What is the vous form of prendre in the imperative?

prompt: Nous ______ la fiche d'identité. [remplir]
hint: Remplir is a regular -ir verb. For nous, remove -ir and add -issons.
explanation: Remplir (to fill in) is a regular -ir verb. For nous: remplir → remplissons. 'We fill in the identity form.'
reviewPrompt: How do you conjugate regular -ir verbs for nous?

prompt: Je ______ une chemise bleue et un pantalon noir pour la soirée. [mettre]
hint: Mettre is irregular: je mets, tu mets, il met...
explanation: Mettre (to put on/wear) is irregular. For je: je mets. 'I am wearing a blue shirt and black trousers for the party.'
reviewPrompt: What are the singular forms of mettre in the present tense?

prompt: Sara et Claudine ______ aller à la fête ce soir. [pouvoir]
hint: Pouvoir with ils/elles: peuvent. Sara et Claudine = elles.
explanation: Pouvoir (to be able to) is irregular. For ils/elles: peuvent. 'Sara and Claudine can go to the party tonight.'
reviewPrompt: What is the ils/elles form of pouvoir?

prompt: Ma mère ______ ses clés. [perdre]
hint: Passé composé with avoir: subject + avoir + past participle. Perdre → perdu.
explanation: Ma mère (she) uses 'a' from avoir. Perdre → perdu (lost). 'My mother lost her keys.' Well done if you got both parts!
reviewPrompt: What is the past participle of perdre?

prompt: Elles finissent leurs exercices. Transforme au passé composé.
hint: Elles + avoir = ont. Finir → fini. Rewrite the whole sentence in the past.
explanation: To transform to passé composé: replace the present tense verb with avoir + past participle. Elles finissent → Elles ont fini. The rest stays the same!
reviewPrompt: How do you form the past participle of -ir verbs like finir?

prompt: Tu ______ cette leçon ? [comprendre]
hint: Tu + avoir = as. Comprendre → compris (irregular past participle).
explanation: Comprendre (to understand) has an irregular past participle: compris. Tu as compris = Did you understand? Great work remembering the irregular!
reviewPrompt: What is the past participle of comprendre?

prompt: Mon ami ______ une lettre à sa grand-mère. [écrire]
hint: Mon ami (he) + avoir = a. Écrire → écrit (irregular past participle).
explanation: Écrire (to write) has an irregular past participle: écrit. 'My friend wrote a letter to his grandmother.' Keep practising those irregular participles!
reviewPrompt: What is the past participle of écrire?

prompt: Elle ______ au cinéma avec ses amis. [aller]
hint: Aller uses être in passé composé. Elle = feminine, so add -e to the past participle.
explanation: Aller is one of the DR MRS VANDERTRAMP verbs that use être. With elle (feminine), the past participle agrees: allé → allée. She went to the cinema!
reviewPrompt: Why does allé become allée here?

prompt: Nous ______ à l'aéroport à temps. [arriver]
hint: Arriver uses être. Nous + être = sommes. Add -s for plural agreement.
explanation: Arriver uses être. Nous sommes arrivés -- the past participle agrees with the plural subject (add -s). 'We arrived at the airport on time.'
reviewPrompt: What agreement do you add to the past participle with nous and être?

prompt: Les élèves ______ tôt ce matin. [partir]
hint: Partir uses être. Les élèves (ils) + être = sont. Partir → parti + s for plural.
explanation: Partir uses être in passé composé. Les élèves = ils, so: sont partis. The -s shows plural agreement. 'The students left early this morning.'
reviewPrompt: What is the past participle of partir?

prompt: Le chat ______ dans la maison. [entrer]
hint: Entrer uses être. Le chat (il) + être = est. Entrer → entré.
explanation: Entrer uses être. Le chat is masculine singular, so no extra agreement needed: est entré. 'The cat entered the house.'
reviewPrompt: Is entrer an avoir or être verb in passé composé?

prompt: Corrige: Nous avons allé au parc.
hint: Aller uses être, not avoir. And don't forget the agreement!
explanation: Aller is a DR MRS VANDERTRAMP verb -- it uses être, not avoir. With nous (plural), the past participle needs -s: sommes allés.
reviewPrompt: Which auxiliary does aller use in the passé composé?

prompt: Corrige: Elle est mangé une pizza.
hint: Manger is NOT a DR MRS VANDERTRAMP verb. It uses avoir.
explanation: Manger uses avoir, not être. Only movement/state-change verbs (DR MRS VANDERTRAMP) use être. Correct: Elle a mangé une pizza.
reviewPrompt: Does manger use avoir or être in passé composé?

prompt: Corrige: Ils sont pris le train.
hint: Prendre uses avoir, not être.
explanation: Prendre (to take) uses avoir in passé composé. Only DR MRS VANDERTRAMP verbs use être. Correct: Ils ont pris le train.
reviewPrompt: Which auxiliary does prendre use?

prompt: Corrige: Tu as tombé dans la cour.
answer: Tu es tombé dans la cour.
hint: Tomber (to fall) is a DR MRS VANDERTRAMP verb -- it uses être!
explanation: Tomber (to fall) uses être because it describes a movement. Tu es tombé = You fell. Well spotted if you caught that!
reviewPrompt: Why does tomber use être instead of avoir?

prompt: Pourquoi M. Lafont fait-il du shopping aujourd'hui ?
answer: Parce que c'est Noël ce weekend et il achète pour sa famille.
options: Parce que c'est Noël ce weekend et il achète pour sa famille.
Parce qu'il a besoin de nouveaux vêtements pour lui-même.
Parce que c'est l'anniversaire de sa femme.
Parce qu'il n'a rien à faire aujourd'hui.
hint: Think about what holiday is coming up and who the gifts are for.
explanation: M. Lafont is shopping because Christmas (Noel) is this weekend and he is buying gifts for his family. Reading comprehension tip: look for time markers and purpose words!
reviewPrompt: What key detail tells you why M. Lafont is shopping?

prompt: For Ma sortie récente, which sequence best matches the uploaded paper?
answer: Where, with whom, what you did, what you wore, what you ate and whether you liked it
options: Where, with whom, what you did, what you wore, what you ate and whether you liked it
What you wore, where, what you ate, with whom, what you did
With whom, what you ate, where, what you wore, what you did
What you did, what you wore, where, with whom, what you ate
hint: Think about the natural order of describing an outing: location first, then companions, activities, and details.
explanation: A good writing frame for 'Ma sortie récente' follows a logical flow: where you went, who you were with, what you did, what you wore, what you ate, and your opinion. This structure helps organise your writing!
reviewPrompt: What is the first thing you should mention when describing a recent outing?

prompt: Quelle robe M. Lafont achète-t-il finalement ?
hint: Look for the colour of the dress he finally decides on.
explanation: M. Lafont finally buys the red dress (la robe rouge) for his wife. When reading, pay attention to adjectives of colour that follow the noun!
reviewPrompt: What colour dress does M. Lafont buy?

prompt: Quels cadeaux achète-t-il pour ses enfants ?
answer: Un jeu vidéo pour son fils et une poupée pour sa fille
options: Un jeu vidéo pour son fils et une poupée pour sa fille
Un livre pour son fils et un vélo pour sa fille
Une poupée pour son fils et un jeu vidéo pour sa fille
hint: Look for what he buys for his son (fils) and daughter (fille) separately.
explanation: He buys a video game (un jeu video) for his son and a doll (une poupee) for his daughter. Reading tip: match each gift to the right child!
reviewPrompt: What does M. Lafont buy for his son?

prompt: Le matin, je ______ à sept heures. [se lever]
hint: Reflexive pronoun changes with subject: je becomes me. Lever has an accent change in some forms.
explanation: Se lever (to get up) is reflexive. With je, the reflexive pronoun is me: je me lève. Notice the accent grave on lève -- the e before a silent ending gets an accent!
reviewPrompt: What reflexive pronoun goes with je?

prompt: À quelle heure est-ce que tu ______ ? [se coucher]
hint: tu + reflexive = te. Coucher is a regular -er verb.
explanation: Se coucher (to go to bed) with tu: tu te couches. The reflexive pronoun for tu is te. 'What time do you go to bed?'
reviewPrompt: What reflexive pronoun goes with tu?

prompt: Le matin, il ______ dans la salle de bains. [se laver]
hint: il/elle + reflexive = se. Laver is a regular -er verb.
explanation: Se laver (to wash oneself) with il: il se lave. The reflexive pronoun for il/elle is se. 'In the morning, he washes in the bathroom.'
reviewPrompt: What reflexive pronoun goes with il/elle?

prompt: Nous ______ avant le petit déjeuner. [s'habiller]
hint: nous + reflexive = nous. So it looks like 'nous nous habillons'. Just fill in the blank part!
explanation: S'habiller (to get dressed) with nous: nous nous habillons. Yes, 'nous' appears twice -- once as subject, once as reflexive pronoun! 'We get dressed before breakfast.'
reviewPrompt: Why does 'nous' appear twice in 'nous nous habillons'?

prompt: Ma sœur ______ à six heures et demie. [se réveiller]
hint: Ma soeur = elle. elle + reflexive = se.
explanation: Se réveiller (to wake up) with elle: elle se réveille. 'My sister wakes up at half past six.' The reflexive pronoun for il/elle is se.
reviewPrompt: What is the difference between se réveiller and se lever?

prompt: What does 'Je me lève à sept heures' mean?
options: I go to bed at 7 o'clock
hint: Se lever means to get up / to rise.
explanation: Se lever means 'to get up'. Je me lève = I get up. Lever comes from the idea of raising yourself. 'I get up at 7 o'clock.'
reviewPrompt: What is the infinitive of 'je me lève'?

prompt: What does 'Je me couche à dix heures' mean?
answer: I go to bed at 10 o'clock
options: I go to bed at 10 o'clock
hint: Se coucher is the opposite of se lever.
explanation: Se coucher means 'to go to bed'. It is the opposite of se lever (to get up). 'I go to bed at 10 o'clock.'
reviewPrompt: What is the opposite of se coucher?

prompt: Les enfants ______ tôt le matin. [se lever]
hint: ils/elles + reflexive = se, and lever has an accent change: lèvent.
explanation: Se lever with ils/elles: se lèvent. The accent grave appears because the ending is silent (-ent is not pronounced). 'The children get up early in the morning.'
reviewPrompt: Why does lever get an accent grave in 'ils se lèvent'?

prompt: Vous ______ avant le petit déjeuner ? [se laver]
hint: vous + reflexive = vous. Fill in the reflexive pronoun + verb.
explanation: Se laver with vous: vous vous lavez. Like nous, the pronoun vous appears twice. 'Do you wash before breakfast?'
reviewPrompt: What reflexive pronoun goes with vous?

prompt: Which is the correct order for a morning routine?
answer: se réveiller → se lever → se laver → s'habiller
options: se réveiller → se lever → se laver → s'habiller
se coucher → se lever → se laver → s'habiller
se laver → se lever → se réveiller → s'habiller
s'habiller → se laver → se lever → se réveiller
hint: Think logically: first you wake up, then you get up, then you wash, then you get dressed.
explanation: The logical order is: se réveiller (wake up) → se lever (get up) → se laver (wash) → s'habiller (get dressed). You can't get dressed before you wake up!
reviewPrompt: What comes first: se réveiller or se lever? What is the difference?

prompt: Write the negative: Je me lève tôt.
answer: Je ne me lève pas tôt.
hint: ne goes BEFORE the reflexive pronoun, pas goes AFTER the verb.
explanation: To negate a reflexive verb: ne goes before the reflexive pronoun, pas goes after the verb. Je me lève → Je ne me lève pas. 'I don't get up early.'
reviewPrompt: Where does 'ne' go in a negative reflexive sentence?

prompt: Write the negative: Elle se baigne.
hint: ne...pas wraps around se + verb: ne se baigne pas.
explanation: Elle se baigne → Elle ne se baigne pas. Remember: ne comes before the reflexive pronoun se, and pas comes after the verb. 'She doesn't swim/bathe.'
reviewPrompt: What does 'se baigner' mean?

prompt: Write the negative: Il se couche tard.
answer: Il ne se couche pas tard.
hint: ne before se, pas after couche. 'Tard' stays at the end.
explanation: Il se couche tard → Il ne se couche pas tard. The word 'tard' (late) stays at the end after 'pas'. 'He doesn't go to bed late.'
reviewPrompt: In a negative reflexive sentence, what goes between ne and pas?

prompt: Which is the correct negative form of 'Tu te lèves' ?
hint: ne goes before the reflexive pronoun, pas goes after the verb.
explanation: The correct order is: subject + ne + reflexive pronoun + verb + pas. So: Tu ne te lèves pas. The ne...pas wraps around the reflexive pronoun and verb together.
reviewPrompt: What is the word order for negating a reflexive verb?

prompt: Je ______ aller au cinéma. [vouloir]
hint: je veux, tu veux, il veut...
explanation: Vouloir (to want) is irregular. Je veux = I want. 'I want to go to the cinema.' After vouloir, use the infinitive of the next verb.
reviewPrompt: What are the je and tu forms of vouloir?

prompt: Nous ______ regarder un film. [vouloir]
hint: nous voulons -- the nous/vous forms keep the -oul- stem.
explanation: Vouloir with nous: nous voulons. The nous and vous forms keep the regular -oul- stem, while je/tu/il use veux/veut. 'We want to watch a film.'
reviewPrompt: How is the nous form of vouloir different from the je form?

prompt: Ils ______ jouer au tennis. [vouloir]
hint: ils/elles veulent -- the stem changes back to veu- for the third person plural.
explanation: Vouloir with ils: ils veulent. The stem changes to veu- (like je veux) but with -lent ending. 'They want to play tennis.'
reviewPrompt: What is the ils/elles form of vouloir?

prompt: Write the negative: Je veux faire mes devoirs.
answer: Je ne veux pas faire mes devoirs.
hint: ne...pas wraps around the conjugated verb (veux), not the infinitive.
explanation: To negate vouloir + infinitive: ne...pas goes around veux. Je ne veux pas faire mes devoirs. The infinitive (faire) stays after pas. 'I don't want to do my homework.'
reviewPrompt: When negating vouloir + infinitive, what does ne...pas wrap around?

prompt: What does 'Qu'est-ce que tu veux faire ?' mean?
answer: What do you want to do?
options: What do you want to do?
hint: Vouloir = to want. Faire = to do.
explanation: 'Qu'est-ce que tu veux faire?' means 'What do you want to do?' Vouloir = to want, faire = to do. This is a very useful question for making plans!
reviewPrompt: How do you ask 'What do you want to do?' in French?

prompt: Which is correct for 'I don't want to go to town'?
answer: Je ne veux pas aller en ville
options: Je ne veux pas aller en ville
Je veux ne pas aller en ville
Je ne veux aller pas en ville
Ne je veux pas aller en ville
hint: ne...pas wraps around the conjugated verb (veux). The infinitive comes after pas.
explanation: ne...pas always wraps around the conjugated verb: Je ne veux pas aller en ville. The infinitive (aller) comes after pas. This is the same pattern for all verb + infinitive constructions!
reviewPrompt: Where does the infinitive go when negating vouloir + infinitive?

prompt: How do you say 'I am average height' in French?
hint: Use être (to be) + de taille moyenne for describing height.
explanation: For height, use être: Je suis de taille moyenne. Other options: Je suis grand(e) (tall), Je suis petit(e) (short), Je suis assez grand(e) (quite tall).
reviewPrompt: How do you describe someone's height in French?

prompt: Describe: She has long blonde hair.
answer: Elle a les cheveux blonds et longs.
hint: Use avoir for hair: j'ai les cheveux... Colour and length are adjectives that agree with cheveux (masculine plural).
explanation: For hair, use avoir: Elle a les cheveux blonds et longs. Remember: cheveux is masculine plural, so adjectives agree: blonds (not blondes), longs (not longues).
reviewPrompt: Do you use avoir or être to describe hair in French?

prompt: How do you say 'I have green eyes'?
hint: Use avoir + les yeux + colour. Eyes (yeux) is masculine plural.
explanation: For eyes, use avoir with 'les': J'ai les yeux verts. Like hair, we use avoir (not être) and the definite article 'les'. Verts agrees with yeux (masculine plural).
reviewPrompt: What verb and article do you use to describe eye colour?

prompt: Say: I am wearing a blue t-shirt and black jeans.
answer: Je porte un tee-shirt bleu et un jean noir.
hint: Use porter for wearing clothes. Colour adjectives go after the noun.
explanation: Porter (to wear) is used for clothes: Je porte un tee-shirt bleu et un jean noir. Colour adjectives come after the noun in French.
reviewPrompt: What verb do you use to say what you are wearing?

prompt: When should you use 'vous' instead of 'tu'?
answer: When talking to adults or more than one person
options: When talking to adults or more than one person
hint: Vous is the polite/formal form and also the plural form.
explanation: Use vous when speaking to adults you don't know well (formal/polite) or when addressing more than one person (plural). Use tu for friends, family, and people your own age.
reviewPrompt: Give an example of when you would use tu and when you would use vous.

prompt: Describe André: tall, brown hair, wearing a jogging and blue t-shirt.
answer: Il est assez grand. Il a les cheveux bruns. Il porte un jogging et un tee-shirt bleu.
hint: Use être for height, avoir for hair, porter for clothes. Three separate sentences.
explanation: The three-step formula: height with être (Il est assez grand), hair with avoir (Il a les cheveux bruns), clothes with porter (Il porte un jogging et un tee-shirt bleu). This pattern works for describing anyone!
reviewPrompt: What are the three verbs used for physical descriptions?

prompt: How do you say 'May I introduce my brother, Marc'?
answer: Je te présente mon frère, Marc
options: Je te présente mon frère, Marc
C'est mon frère il s'appelle Marc
hint: 'Je te présente...' is the polite way to introduce someone to a friend.
explanation: 'Je te présente mon frère, Marc' is the standard way to introduce someone. 'Je te présente' literally means 'I present to you'. Use 'Je vous présente' in formal situations.
reviewPrompt: How would you introduce someone formally vs informally?

prompt: How do you say 'She wears glasses'?
hint: Use porter for accessories too, not just clothes.
explanation: Elle porte des lunettes. We use porter for wearing glasses, just like clothes. 'Des lunettes' means glasses -- always plural in French, like in English!
reviewPrompt: What verb do you use for wearing glasses in French?

prompt: Describe yourself: short, curly black hair, wearing a red dress.
answer: Je suis petit(e). J'ai les cheveux noirs et frisés. Je porte une robe rouge.
hint: Height (être) then hair (avoir) then clothes (porter). Three sentences.
explanation: Follow the formula: Je suis petit(e) (height with être), J'ai les cheveux noirs et frisés (hair with avoir), Je porte une robe rouge (clothes with porter). The (e) depends on whether you are male or female!
reviewPrompt: What does 'frisé' mean when describing hair?

prompt: What does 'les cheveux châtains' mean?
hint: Châtain comes from the word for chestnut (châtaigne).
explanation: Les cheveux châtains means brown/chestnut hair. It comes from 'châtaigne' (chestnut). Other hair colours: blonds (blonde), roux (red/ginger), noirs (black), bruns (dark brown).
reviewPrompt: What is the difference between brun and châtain for hair colour?

prompt: What does 'hier' mean?
hint: This is one of the most common past time expressions.
explanation: 'Hier' means yesterday. It is the most common time marker for the passé composé. Other useful ones: avant-hier (day before yesterday), hier soir (last night).
reviewPrompt: How do you say 'yesterday evening' in French?

prompt: Match: 'la semaine dernière'
hint: Dernière means last (feminine form, agreeing with semaine).
explanation: 'La semaine dernière' means 'last week'. Dernier/dernière means last. Similarly: le mois dernier (last month), l'année dernière (last year).
reviewPrompt: How do you say 'last month' in French?

prompt: Hier, nous ______ la ville. [visiter]
hint: nous + avoir = avons, visiter → visité (remove -er, add -é).
explanation: Passé composé = avoir + past participle. Nous avons visité. Visiter is a regular -er verb: remove -er, add -é. 'Yesterday, we visited the town.'
reviewPrompt: How do you form the past participle of visiter?

prompt: J'______ des cartes postales. [acheter]
hint: je + avoir = ai, acheter → acheté.
explanation: J'ai acheté des cartes postales. With je, avoir becomes ai (and j' before a vowel). Acheter → acheté. 'I bought some postcards.'
reviewPrompt: Why does 'je ai' become 'j'ai'?

prompt: À midi, on ______ un sandwich. [manger]
hint: on + avoir = a, manger → mangé.
explanation: On a mangé un sandwich. 'On' uses the same form of avoir as il/elle: a. Manger → mangé. 'At lunchtime, we ate a sandwich.'
reviewPrompt: What form of avoir does 'on' take?

prompt: Translate to French: He ate a sandwich.
hint: Two parts: avoir in present tense + past participle. He = Il, ate = a mangé.
explanation: Il a mangé un sandwich. The passé composé always has two parts: the auxiliary (avoir/être in present tense) + the past participle. Well done!
reviewPrompt: What are the two parts of the passé composé?

prompt: Translate to French: Did you buy something?
hint: tu + as + past participle. 'Something' = quelque chose.
explanation: Tu as acheté quelque chose? In French, you can make a question just by adding a question mark (and raising your voice). 'Quelque chose' = something.
reviewPrompt: How do you say 'something' in French?

prompt: What does 'ce matin' mean?
hint: 'Ce' means this, 'matin' means morning.
explanation: 'Ce matin' means 'this morning'. 'Ce' = this, 'matin' = morning. Similarly: ce soir (this evening), cet après-midi (this afternoon).
reviewPrompt: How do you say 'this evening' in French?

prompt: Hier soir, ils ______ un film. [regarder]
hint: ils + avoir = ont, regarder → regardé.
explanation: Ils ont regardé un film. With ils/elles, avoir becomes ont. Regarder → regardé (regular -er pattern). 'Last night, they watched a film.'
reviewPrompt: What is the ils/elles form of avoir?

prompt: L'après-midi, on ______ au tennis. [jouer]
hint: on + avoir = a, jouer → joué.
explanation: On a joué au tennis. Jouer → joué (regular -er verb). Remember: 'jouer à' is used for sports and games. 'In the afternoon, we played tennis.'
reviewPrompt: What preposition follows jouer when talking about sports?

prompt: How do you form the past participle of -er verbs?
hint: The pattern is simple and the same for ALL -er verbs.
explanation: For -er verbs, remove the -er ending and add -é. Danser → dansé, manger → mangé, jouer → joué. This is the most common pattern and works for all regular -er verbs!
reviewPrompt: What is the past participle pattern for -er verbs?

prompt: Complete: On a ______ sur la musique. [danser]
hint: danser → dansé (remove -er, add -é).
explanation: Danser → dansé. Remove -er, add -é. On a dansé sur la musique = We danced to the music. All -er verbs follow this same pattern!
reviewPrompt: What is the past participle of danser?

prompt: Complete: Ils ont ______ de la musique. [écouter]
hint: écouter → écouté (same -er pattern).
explanation: Écouter → écouté. Same pattern: remove -er, add -é. Ils ont écouté de la musique = They listened to music. Keep practising this pattern!
reviewPrompt: What is the past participle of écouter?

prompt: Pour la fête, Thomas a ______ son nouveau jean. [porter]
hint: porter → porté.
explanation: Porter → porté. Thomas a porté son nouveau jean = Thomas wore his new jeans for the party. The past participle tells us this already happened.
reviewPrompt: What is the difference between 'il porte' and 'il a porté'?

prompt: Thomas a ______ Nicole à sa fête. [inviter]
hint: inviter → invité.
explanation: Inviter → invité. Thomas a invité Nicole à sa fête = Thomas invited Nicole to his party. Another perfect example of the -er → -é pattern.
reviewPrompt: What is the past participle of inviter?

prompt: Mme Martin a ______ le gâteau. [préparer]
hint: préparer → préparé.
explanation: Préparer → préparé. Mme Martin a préparé le gâteau = Mrs Martin prepared the cake. The accent on the é in the stem stays throughout!
reviewPrompt: What is the past participle of préparer?

prompt: Write in French: We bought chips and cola.
answer: Nous avons acheté des chips et du coca.
hint: Subject + avoir + past participle + object. We = Nous, bought = avons acheté.
explanation: Nous avons acheté des chips et du coca. Remember: 'des' for plural items (chips), 'du' for uncountable (cola). The passé composé structure stays the same!
reviewPrompt: When do you use 'des' vs 'du' before food items?

prompt: Write in French: I danced with my friends.
hint: j'ai + past participle. With = avec, my friends = mes amis.
explanation: J'ai dansé avec mes amis. J'ai (I have) + dansé (danced) + avec mes amis (with my friends). You're getting great at building passé composé sentences!
reviewPrompt: How do you say 'with my friends' in French?

prompt: Sophie a ______ le salon. [ranger]
hint: ranger → rangé (tidy up).
explanation: Ranger → rangé. Sophie a rangé le salon = Sophie tidied up the living room. Ranger means to tidy/put away -- very useful vocab!
reviewPrompt: What does ranger mean?

prompt: Which sentence is correct passé composé?
answer: J'ai regardé un film hier soir
options: J'ai regardé un film hier soir
Je regardé un film hier soir
J'ai regarder un film hier soir
Je suis regardé un film hier soir
hint: Passé composé needs TWO parts: avoir/être + past participle. Don't forget either part!
explanation: J'ai regardé un film hier soir is correct. Common mistakes: forgetting avoir (je regardé), using the infinitive instead of past participle (j'ai regarder), or using être when it should be avoir (je suis regardé).
reviewPrompt: What are the three most common passé composé mistakes to avoid?
